headline: Iran war enters Day 5 with leadership vacuum and Hormuz closure triggering KOSPI's worst crash in history, record gold at $5,413, and global stagflation fears

top_stories.title: Iran Under Active Bombardment: Supreme Leader Killed, State in Freefall
top_stories.summary: US-Israeli Operation Epic Fury/Roaring Lion continues into Day 5, with strikes targeting remaining IRGC command infrastructure and nuclear facilities including Natanz. Supreme Leader Khamenei was killed on Feb 28, leaving the head of state position vacant with no constitutional succession mechanism activated. An IRGC Interim Leadership Council is governing. The economy is in freefall: GDP on a -10% to -15% annualized trajectory, inflation at 42.4% approaching hyperinflation, the rial collapsing on parallel markets, and the political risk premium raised to 5,200 bps -- the highest in the tracked universe.
top_stories.investor_impact: Iran is completely uninvestable across all asset classes and timeframes. The primary investor concern is indirect: Hormuz closure duration determines global energy price trajectory and Gulf sovereign creditworthiness.

top_stories.title: Strait of Hormuz Effectively Closed: 20-30% of Global Oil Supply Disrupted
top_stories.summary: The Strait of Hormuz remains closed to commercial shipping, blocking approximately 21 million barrels per day of oil and 150 bcm/year of LNG. Combined with continued Houthi disruption of Red Sea/Suez Canal traffic, both major Middle East maritime chokepoints are simultaneously closed for the first time in modern history. Gulf producers (SAU, ARE, KWT, QAT, OMN, IRQ) face severe export constraints. VLCC rates are at all-time highs. Cape of Good Hope rerouting adds 2-3 weeks to transit times.
top_stories.investor_impact: The most severe global energy supply disruption in decades. Brent at $80 (+36% YTD) with Goldman targeting $110 and JPMorgan seeing $120-130 if disruptions persist. Energy importers face stagflation; non-Hormuz producers (NOR, BRA, KAZ, AZE) are primary beneficiaries.

top_stories.title: KOSPI Crashes 12.1% -- Worst Day in History; Global Equity Selloff
top_stories.summary: South Korea's KOSPI plunged 12.1% on March 4, the worst single-day crash in the index's history, surpassing the previous day's 7.24% decline. Samsung fell 12% and SK Hynix dropped 10%. The crash exposed Korea's critical structural vulnerability: 70% of its oil imports transit the now-closed Strait of Hormuz. The selloff spread globally with DAX -4.1%, Nikkei -3.6%, FTSE -3.4%, and Bovespa -3.2%. The S&P 500 was the sole major index to finish positive (+0.83%).
top_stories.investor_impact: Korean assets face significant near-term headwinds. KOSPI recovery depends on Hormuz resolution timeline. Won depreciation at 1,483/USD creates potential entry points for USD-based investors if Hormuz reopens. Gold at $5,413 (record) confirms safe-haven rotation.

top_stories.title: US Trade Policy Constitutional Crisis: SCOTUS Ruling Forces Section 122 Pivot
top_stories.summary: The US Supreme Court struck down IEEPA-based tariffs 6-3 on February 20, invalidating the legal foundation of the Liberation Day tariff regime. The administration pivoted to Section 122 with a 15% global surcharge (effective immediately, 150-day clock) and imposed an additional 10% tariff on China effective March 4, bringing the total China tariff to approximately 35%. Companies are suing for $134B+ in refunds from previously collected IEEPA tariffs. Consumer inflation expectations (UMich 5-year) hit 4.1%, the highest since 1995.
top_stories.investor_impact: The 150-day Section 122 deadline creates a policy cliff in late July. The $134B+ refund litigation introduces fiscal uncertainty. Tariff rate dropped from 16% to 9.1%, temporarily improving trade openness, but the new China tariffs compound inflationary pressure from the oil shock.

top_stories.title: China-Japan Relations Collapse: Rare Earth Ban and 50-Company Sanctions
top_stories.summary: China sanctioned 50 Japanese companies on February 25 and imposed rare earth and dual-use goods export controls in response to PM Takaichi's Taiwan defense comments. Japan imports 60% of its rare earth from China, and the ban directly disrupts defense and advanced manufacturing supply chains. This is the worst bilateral moment in Japan-China relations in decades. Combined with the Hormuz oil shock (Japan has only 10% energy independence), Japan faces a dual supply chain crisis. The Nikkei fell 3.61%.
top_stories.investor_impact: Japan's defense sector accelerates domestic supply chain development. Rare earth substitution and diversification become urgent. Japanese manufacturers with China exposure face margin compression. Long-term structural shift toward supply chain onshoring in Japan.

regional_summaries.North America: The US prosecutes the largest military operation since Iraq 2003 while navigating a constitutional trade policy crisis. SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs, forcing a Section 122 pivot with a 150-day clock. The Fed holds at 3.50-3.75% facing stagflation risk as oil prices surge. 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico took effect March 4 alongside 10% additional on China. Protests in 23 US cities drew 847 arrests over the Iran war. Canada's energy sector benefits from the oil price surge, partly offsetting tariff damage. Mexico faces tariff headwinds but Pemex benefits from elevated crude prices.
regional_summaries.Europe: European markets sold off heavily (DAX -4.1%, FTSE -3.4%) as the oil shock compounds energy security concerns. Germany faces stagflation risk with 30% energy independence and additional import costs of approximately $45B/year at current oil prices. NATO achieved a historic milestone with all 32 members meeting the 2% GDP defense spending target. The EU approved an additional EUR 20B Ukraine support package. European defense stocks outperform. The ECB faces a policy dilemma between growth support and inflation containment. Ukraine gained approximately 120 km2 in Zaporizhzhia and struck Russian Caspian oil infrastructure with Flamingo missiles.
regional_summaries.East Asia: The region suffered the worst of the Hormuz fallout. KOSPI's -12.1% crash was the worst in history, exposing Korea's 70% oil dependency on Hormuz routes. China imposed rare earth and dual-use export controls on Japan, the worst bilateral moment in decades. China's NPC opens March 5 with stimulus expected as GDP decelerates from 4.8% to 4.2%. China also faces internal instability with 19 PLA senior officers purged -- the largest military purge since 2015. Japan's LDP supermajority under PM Takaichi enables defense expansion to $58B but triggered the Chinese sanctions. Taiwan's 5% energy independence makes it the most vulnerable economy in the dataset.
regional_summaries.South Asia: Pakistan declared open war on Afghanistan on February 27, launching airstrikes on Kabul and Kandahar. Domestic protests killed 20. The military deployed to Karachi, Lahore, and FATA border areas. Political risk premium at 2,050 bps. India, importing 85% of its oil via Hormuz-dependent routes, faces cost-push inflation rising from 2.8% to 4% and current account deterioration. India remains the fastest-growing major economy (6.2%) but faces acute energy cost headwinds. Bangladesh's BNP won a landslide election, marking a democratic transition.
regional_summaries.Middle East & North Africa: The region is in its most severe crisis in decades. Iran is under active bombardment with its supreme leader killed. IRGC struck US bases and Gulf states across the region. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia all sustained direct Iranian missile and drone attacks. Hormuz closure blocks Gulf oil and LNG exports. Saudi PETROLINE partially diverts exports but revenue loss is massive. Hezbollah broke its ceasefire with Israel on March 1, reopening the Lebanon front. Israel faces multi-front war with military spending at 8.8% of GDP. Egypt loses approximately $800M/month in Suez Canal revenue from Houthi disruption. Turkey positions as potential mediator.
regional_summaries.Sub-Saharan Africa: South Africa announced a R1 trillion infrastructure plan, the largest in the country's history. DRC-Rwanda received new sanctions from the US and EU. Ethiopia remains in selective default (SD/Ca/RD) with Tigray conflict resumption threatening the peace agreement; elections scheduled for June 1. Nigeria faces a Boko Haram massacre (170+ killed), 23% inflation, and labor protests. Ghana remains in selective default with debt restructuring ongoing. Sudan's SAF retook central Khartoum -- the largest government territorial gain since April 2023 -- but 24.6 million face acute food insecurity.
regional_summaries.Latin America: Brazil benefits from its position as a net oil exporter geographically insulated from the Hormuz crisis, though Bovespa fell 3.2% in the global risk-off. The EU-Mercosur Partnership Agreement (signed January 17) provides structural trade diversification. Argentina shows dramatic inflation deceleration (monthly rate at 2.9%) under Milei reforms, with landmark labor reform passed by the Senate. Peru installs its 9th president in a decade ahead of April 12 elections. Mexico faces 25% US tariffs but oil revenues provide an offset.
regional_summaries.Central Asia: Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan are among the primary beneficiaries of the Hormuz crisis, with their pipeline routes (CPC and BTC respectively) bypassing the disrupted strait. Oil price surges deliver windfall revenues. Uzbekistan continues institutional reform trajectory. The region's relative insulation from Middle East conflict creates a comparative investment advantage.
regional_summaries.Southeast Asia: Myanmar's military junta controls only 21% of territory, down from 33% in mid-2024, with GDP at -5.8% and inflation at 32%. The country is effectively a failed state. The Philippines faces a governance crisis from the Marcos corruption scandal with legislative leaders forced out. Singapore's chokepoint exposure rose to 0.98 as a trade hub dependent on Middle East shipping routes. Vietnam and Thailand face imported inflation from the oil shock.
regional_summaries.Oceania: Australia benefits from the Hormuz crisis as a non-Hormuz LNG producer with windfall pricing as Qatari supply (20% of global LNG) goes offline. Gold mining revenues surge at $5,413/oz. The ASX 200 fell 1.94% in the broader selloff but outperformed most peers. New Zealand faces imported inflation from elevated energy costs.

market_context.summary: Global markets are in crisis mode driven by the Hormuz closure and Iran war. The KOSPI -12.1% crash was the worst in the index's history. Brent crude at $80 (+36% YTD) with analyst targets of $110-130 if disruptions persist. Gold hit an all-time record of $5,413/oz on safe-haven flows. The S&P 500 was the sole major index to post a weekly gain (+0.83%), reflecting relative US insulation as a domestic energy producer.

notable_moves.driver: Worst day in history; Korea's 70% Hormuz oil dependency exposed
European energy shock from Hormuz closure and defense spending pressure
China rare earth ban + Hormuz oil shock (10% energy independence)
Iran crisis risk-off; energy importer vulnerability
Global risk-off despite Brazil's oil exporter positioning
Relative US insulation as domestic energy producer; defense sector rally
Hormuz closure blocking 21 mbpd; YTD change reflects sustained disruption
Record $5,413/oz on safe-haven flows; geopolitical risk premium at maximum
$85/oz driven by safe-haven demand and industrial scarcity concerns
4.08% yield; flight to quality offset by inflation expectations

watchlist_updates.summary: Moved to maximum risk. Head of state VACANT, PRP 5,200 bps, GDP trajectory -10% to -15%, hyperinflation imminent. Uninvestable.
KOSPI -12.1% worst day ever. PRP doubled to 120 bps. SCCE raised to 0.88. Energy dependency vulnerability fully exposed.
Upgraded to critical. Multi-front war with Hezbollah ceasefire collapse and IRGC retaliation. Military spending 8.8% GDP. Budget deadline March 31.
Dual supply chain crisis: China rare earth ban + Hormuz oil shock. SCCE raised to 0.85. Nikkei -3.61%. Defense spending at $58B.
First non-zero PRP (75 bps) in platform history. Trade policy constitutional crisis. Oil shock pushing inflation higher. Protests in 23 cities.
Primary beneficiary of Hormuz crisis as North Sea producer with non-Hormuz export routes. Oil windfall revenues.
Net oil exporter, geographically insulated from Hormuz. EU-Mercosur FTA signed. Despite Bovespa -3.2%, structural positioning strong.
Monthly inflation decelerating to 2.9%. Senate passed landmark labor reform. Milei reform trajectory positive despite CCC+/Caa1 credit.

data_quality_summary.notes: All Iran economic data is extremely low confidence under active conflict conditions. Market data is real-time with high confidence. Energy dependency calculations based on established trade flow data. 7 volatility flags triggered across equity and commodity markets. Missing data: EGP, NGN exchange rates; JSE Top 40 and TSX indices; several Tier 2/3 bond yields.
